source.title: Ifa Divination Trays from Isale-Oyo

licence.label: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0
licence.scopeNote: Quoted text is reused non-commercially with attribution and without silent editorial normalization. Share-alike applies to adapted quoted material.

source.translationCredit: English translations as printed by Pogoson and Akande (2011).
source.transcriptionNote: The Yorùbá and English lines are reproduced as printed. The paper does not consistently print tone marks; Online Ifá has not silently modernized the orthography.
source.reviewNote: The passages are source-verified quotations attributed by the article to named Isale-Ọyọ diviners. They have not yet received a separate lineage or practitioner review inside Online Ifá.

verses.context: A verse of Ọ̀yẹ̀kú Méjì about the killing of a great leopard and the sharing of its body between kings. The article records it while explaining big cats carved on divination trays.
yoruba: Oni ni won roko leti opon
Oni la o kun Ifa Olukorokorobojo
Ola la o kun Ifa Olukorokorobojo
Ani won o kun, Won kun
Won gbe ori re fun Oba Ido
Won gbe yegese ara re fun Oba Eleyo Geji
english: Today we shall clear the forest on the edges of the tray
Tomorrow we shall fry bean cake in a gourd (impossibilities) …
Today we will divide it, a divination of Olukorokorobojo
Tomorrow we will cut it to pieces, a divination of Olukorokorobojo
We asked them to butcher it, they did
They killed a big tiger
They cover it and kept it under the plantain trees
They gave its head to the king of Ido
The son of Ejiworogbe of Ido
Another part was given to King Eleyo Geji
The one that thrives on sacrifice

verses.context: The opening of the Ogbè Òfún story in which Èṣù tests Ọ̀rúnmìlà with a borrowed monkey. The article records it while discussing Èṣù's face on the head of a divination tray.
yoruba: Adia fun Esu on gbogbo irunmole ti won jo nsore
Adia fun Orunmila oun Esu odara ti won jo nsore
english: Ogbe return what was kept in your custody to the owner
No, I will not return it
The same performed divination for Esu and all other divinities who were friends
It also performed divination for Orunmila and Esu who were friends

verses.context: The verse of the Snake: harmless at creation and used for tying firewood, the snake sacrifices and receives its venom. The article records it while explaining snakes carved on tray borders.
yoruba: Itakun ni ejo, won fi ndigi lati oko wale
english: Okaran Osa is the divination for the Snake
The Snake consulted ifa for solution
He was amidst enemies
At the time of creation
Snake was harmless
Snake was used for tying firewood
Snake could no longer contain this insult, he went to the diviner
He was asked to sacrifice in honour of his head
He performed the sacrifice
He was asked to sacrifice needle
He did
Snake became poisonous with venom
It became frightful
A snake has only one head
Yet, its appearance sends several heads restless

verses.context: From Ògúndá Ọ̀wọ́nrín (also called Ogundanlare): a pilgrim's plea to Ifá for the prosperity given to others, ending with the honour of a horse. The article records it while explaining horsemen carved in Ifá art.
yoruba: Emi ko ri eni ti nrele Alara
Ko ba mi ki gbogbo omo won nile Alara
Emi o ri eni tin lo si ode Ejigbo
Ko ba mi ki Orisatalabi ara ode Ejigbo …
Ifa ise re ni mo je kiri, n go je ngo mu
Ti mo fi de Oke Ijeti ile Agboniregun
Won ni iwo lo se fun Olu ti Olu fi lowo
Ifa ire ni ko se fun mi ki nlowo …
Esin ni won fi ru oku Owarangun Aga wale
Ti mo ba gbogbogbo, ti mo ba tototo
Esin ni ki omo o fi ru oku mi dan dan dan
english: Whoever is going to Alara
Should salute inhabitants of Alara
Whoever is going to Ejigbo
Should salute Orisatalabi the son of Ejigbo …
Ifa it is your instructions that I am carrying out, that made me not eat nor drink
Till I got to Oke Ijeti the abode of Agboniregun
I overheard that you (Ifa) helped Olu to make plenty money
Ifa favour me also to make money …
The day Owarangun died
His corpse was transported home on a horse
Ifa, when I am old and aged
Let my children carry my corpse with horses

supplementaryPassages.recordedFrom: A closing pattern described by Pogoson and Akande
supplementaryPassages.context: The article gives these lines as a pattern used to close many narratives after the prescribed sacrifice has been performed. Online Ifá does not treat it as a universal close or prescribe an offering.
yoruba: Won ni ebo ni o waa ru.
Lo ba dipe ohun gbogbo nlo deede.
Orin awo wa bo si lenu…
english: He took his problems to ifa
He was told to perform sacrifice,
And he performed it.
After he performed the sacrifice
He became a happy man.
He started to sing the song of ifa priests…
